title: Demand-side policies: fiscal and monetary policy
summary: When an economy slips into recession or overheats with inflation, policymakers reach for demand-side policies: fiscal policy (run by the government through spending and taxation) and monetary policy (run by an independent central bank through interest rates). This lesson builds both tools from scratch, shows how each shifts aggregate demand to close a deflationary or inflationary gap on an AD/AS diagram, and - crucially - trains the balanced evaluation that separates a top-band Paper 1 part (b) answer from an average one.
learningObjectives: Explain fiscal policy - its tools (government spending and taxation), the budget balance, expansionary versus contractionary stances, and automatic stabilisers.
Explain monetary policy - the policy interest rate, the role and independence of the central bank, expansionary versus contractionary stances, and the transmission mechanism to aggregate demand.
Use the AD/AS model to show how each policy closes a deflationary (recessionary) gap or an inflationary gap, labelling the shift in AD and its effect on real output and the price level.

Evaluate the strengths and limitations of fiscal and monetary policy - including time lags, crowding out, government debt, the liquidity trap and political constraints - and reach a reasoned, balanced judgement.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL; the multiplier is HL.

simpleExplanation.title: Two Sets of Hands on the Economy's Controls
simpleExplanation.summary: Demand-side policies manage the total spending in an economy. The government uses its budget (fiscal policy); the central bank uses interest rates (monetary policy). Both aim to keep the economy near full employment with stable prices - speeding it up in a slump, slowing it down when it overheats.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture the economy as a shower. The GOVERNMENT controls the flow with fiscal policy - turning the tap on (spending more, taxing less) fills the room faster; turning it down (spending less, taxing more) slows it. The CENTRAL BANK controls the temperature with monetary policy - lowering interest rates makes borrowing cheaper and spending 'hotter'; raising them cools everything down. Get either wrong and you are scalded by inflation or left shivering in recession.
steps: Diagnose the gap. A deflationary (recessionary) gap means output is below full employment and unemployment is high; an inflationary gap means output is above full employment and prices are rising.
Pick the policy and the stance. Government -> fiscal (spending, taxes); central bank -> monetary (interest rates). Use EXPANSIONARY policy to fight a slump, CONTRACTIONARY policy to fight inflation.
Trace it through AD/AS. Expansionary policy shifts AD right (raising output and the price level); contractionary policy shifts AD left (lowering the price level and output).
Evaluate. No tool is perfect - weigh strengths against limitations (lags, crowding out, debt, the liquidity trap, political pressure) before judging which policy fits the situation.

flashcards.back: Policies that deliberately shift aggregate demand to influence output, employment and the price level. They come in two families: fiscal (government) and monetary (central bank).
The use of government spending (G) and taxation (T) to influence aggregate demand. Set by the GOVERNMENT, usually through an annual budget.
The central bank's use of the policy interest rate (and the money supply) to influence aggregate demand. Set by the CENTRAL BANK, which is usually independent of the government.

The difference between government revenue and spending in a year. A budget DEFICIT means G > T (typical of expansionary policy); a budget SURPLUS means T > G (typical of contractionary policy); balanced means G = T.
Raising government spending and/or cutting taxes to boost aggregate demand and close a deflationary gap. Tends to widen the budget deficit.
Cutting government spending and/or raising taxes to reduce aggregate demand and close an inflationary gap. Tends to move the budget toward surplus.

Features of the budget that dampen the cycle WITHOUT any new decision: in a slump, tax revenue falls and unemployment benefit spending rises (cushioning AD); in a boom the reverse happens. They act instantly, with no policy lag.

An initial injection into the economy raises income, part of which is re-spent, raising income again, and so on. Multiplier k = 1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / MPS. So a given rise in G produces a larger total rise in real GDP. HL only.
The interest rate the central bank sets and charges commercial banks. It anchors the whole structure of borrowing and saving rates in the economy - the main lever of monetary policy.
Keeping interest-rate decisions out of politicians' hands so they target long-run price stability rather than short-run electoral gain. This makes anti-inflation commitments credible.

How a change in the policy rate reaches AD: it alters market lending/saving rates -> changes borrowing, consumption (C) and investment (I) -> also affects asset prices, the exchange rate and confidence -> shifts AD.
Limitation of expansionary fiscal policy: government borrowing to fund a deficit can push up interest rates, reducing private investment and consumption - partly offsetting the intended boost to AD.

The delay between a problem, the policy response and its effect. Fiscal policy suffers long DECISION lags (budgets, legislation); monetary policy is decided fast but its IMPACT on AD can take many months.
Limitation of expansionary monetary policy: when rates are already near zero and confidence is very low, further cuts fail to revive borrowing and spending, so monetary policy loses traction.

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is a tool of MONETARY policy, not fiscal policy?
In most economies, who sets the policy interest rate?
An economy is in a boom with an inflationary gap and rising prices. The appropriate demand-side response is:
options: Expansionary monetary policy: cut interest rates
Contractionary policy: raise taxes and/or raise interest rates
Do nothing, because booms always self-correct instantly
quickCheck.answer: Contractionary policy: raise taxes and/or raise interest rates

quickCheck.prompt: Which body decides fiscal policy (government spending and taxation)?

options: Government borrowing raising interest rates and reducing private investment
Consumers rushing to spend before prices rise
quickCheck.answer: Government borrowing raising interest rates and reducing private investment

faq.q: What is the single clearest difference between fiscal and monetary policy?
faq.a: WHO runs it and WITH WHAT. Fiscal policy is the GOVERNMENT changing its own spending and taxation. Monetary policy is the CENTRAL BANK changing the policy interest rate (and money supply). If a question involves interest rates, it is monetary; if it involves the budget, taxes or government spending, it is fiscal. Both, however, work by shifting aggregate demand.

faq.q: Why does central bank independence matter?
faq.a: A government facing an election has an incentive to cut interest rates for a short-lived boom, even if inflation follows. Handing rate-setting to an independent central bank with a clear price-stability mandate removes that temptation, so its promises to control inflation are believed. Credible expectations of low inflation are themselves stabilising, which is a large part of why independence works.

faq.q: If both policies can shift AD, why not just always use monetary policy - it acts faster?
faq.a: Monetary policy is decided quickly and is easily reversible, which are real strengths. But it is a blunt instrument that hits the whole economy, and in a deep slump with rates near zero it can lose traction (the liquidity trap). Fiscal policy can be targeted at the sectors or regions that need it and works even when rates cannot fall further - but it is slow to legislate, adds to government debt and can crowd out private investment. Because each covers the other's weaknesses, they are often coordinated rather than used alone.

sections.content: Demand-side policies are the main levers policymakers pull to steer the business cycle. When output falls below the full-employment level and unemployment climbs, or when spending races ahead of capacity and prices surge, governments and central banks step in to nudge aggregate demand back toward a stable path. This lesson builds both tools - fiscal and monetary - shows exactly how each closes a deflationary or inflationary gap on the AD/AS diagram, and then does the harder work examiners reward most: weighing each policy's strengths against its limitations and reaching a balanced judgement.
Fiscal policy: the government's budget as a lever

Fiscal policy is the government changing its own spending and taxation to influence aggregate demand. Recall that AD = C + I + G + (X - M). Government spending (G) is a component of AD directly, while taxation (T) works indirectly by changing the disposable income households have to consume (C) and the profits firms retain to invest (I). Because the government controls both G and T through its budget, it can push AD in whichever direction the economy needs.

items: **Government spending (G):** on infrastructure, healthcare, education, public-sector wages and transfer payments. Raising it injects demand directly.
**Taxation (T):** direct taxes (income, corporation) and indirect taxes (VAT/GST). Cutting taxes leaves households and firms with more to spend and invest.

**The budget balance:** if G > T the government runs a DEFICIT (borrowing to cover the gap); if T > G it runs a SURPLUS; if G = T the budget is balanced. Expansionary fiscal policy tends to widen a deficit; contractionary policy moves toward surplus.
**Who decides:** the GOVERNMENT, through the budget process (e.g. the finance ministry / treasury and the legislature). This is the key contrast with monetary policy.

sections.content: The stance of fiscal policy depends on the problem. To close a DEFLATIONARY (recessionary) gap, the government uses EXPANSIONARY fiscal policy - raising G and/or cutting T - shifting AD to the right. To close an INFLATIONARY gap, it uses CONTRACTIONARY fiscal policy - cutting G and/or raising T - shifting AD to the left.

items: **Automatic stabilisers** soften the cycle with no new decision at all. In a downturn, incomes fall so tax revenue automatically drops, while spending on unemployment benefits automatically rises - both cushion the fall in AD. In a boom the reverse happens, restraining AD. Because they need no legislation, they have no decision lag.
**Discretionary fiscal policy**, by contrast, is a deliberate new choice to change spending or taxes - more powerful but slower, because it must be decided and passed.

sections.content: **HL — the multiplier.** An initial injection does not stop where it lands. New government spending becomes someone's income; they spend a fraction of it (the marginal propensity to consume, MPC), which becomes another person's income, and so on. The multiplier is k = 1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / MPS, so the final rise in real GDP is LARGER than the initial injection. The multiplier is HL content; SL students should still understand that fiscal injections have knock-on effects.

sections.question: [HL] An economy is in a deflationary gap. The government raises spending on transport infrastructure by $20 billion. The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.75. (a) Calculate the value of the multiplier. (b) Calculate the maximum total increase in real GDP. (c) State one reason the actual increase might be smaller.
sections.solution:
**(a) The multiplier.**
k = 1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / (1 - 0.75) = 1 / 0.25 = **4**.
So every $1 of new spending can raise total GDP by up to $4.

**(b) Total increase in real GDP.**
Total change = initial injection x multiplier = $20bn x 4 = **$80 billion**.

**(c) Why the actual figure may be smaller.**
The multiplier assumes no offsetting leakages or reactions. In practice the boost may be reduced by **crowding out** (government borrowing pushing up interest rates and reducing private investment), by higher **imports** as incomes rise (leaking demand abroad), or by a **partly full-employment economy**, where extra demand raises prices rather than real output. Any of these means the real GDP rise is below the $80bn theoretical maximum.

sections.content: Monetary policy: the central bank and the interest rate

Monetary policy is run by the central bank, which in most modern economies is independent of the elected government. Its main tool is the policy (base) interest rate - the rate at which commercial banks borrow from it. Because every other borrowing and saving rate in the economy is built on top of the policy rate, moving it ripples out to mortgages, business loans and savings returns. The central bank usually targets a stated inflation rate (commonly around 2%) and adjusts the policy rate to hit it.

items: **Independence:** decisions sit with the central bank, not politicians. This stops the government cutting rates for a pre-election boom at the cost of later inflation, and makes the bank's commitment to low inflation credible - which itself helps anchor expectations.
**Expansionary (loose) monetary policy:** to close a deflationary gap, the bank LOWERS the policy rate. Cheaper borrowing encourages consumption (C) and investment (I), shifting AD right.
**Contractionary (tight) monetary policy:** to close an inflationary gap, the bank RAISES the policy rate. Dearer borrowing and better returns to saving reduce C and I, shifting AD left.

sections.content: How a rate change actually reaches aggregate demand is the transmission mechanism - a chain worth spelling out in an exam because examiners reward the explanation of WHY, not just the direction. A CUT in the policy rate: (1) lowers market lending rates on loans and mortgages; (2) makes borrowing cheaper and saving less rewarding, so households borrow and spend more (C rises) and firms find more investment projects profitable (I rises); (3) can lift asset prices and confidence, and weaken the exchange rate, boosting net exports; (4) the combined effect shifts AD to the right, raising real output and the price level. A rate RISE runs the same chain in reverse.

sections.question: Inflation is running at 7%, well above the central bank's 2% target, and the economy has an inflationary gap. State the appropriate monetary policy and analyse, step by step, its effect on aggregate demand, the price level and real output, referring to an AD/AS diagram.
sections.solution:
**Policy:** contractionary (tight) monetary policy - the central bank RAISES the policy interest rate.

**Step-by-step transmission:**
1. **Market rates rise.** Commercial banks pass the higher policy rate on, so mortgages, loans and credit become more expensive and saving more rewarding.
2. **Consumption (C) falls.** Households borrow less for big purchases and are rewarded for postponing spending, so C decreases.
3. **Investment (I) falls.** Projects that were profitable at low rates are now cancelled or delayed because borrowing costs more, so I decreases.
4. **Aggregate demand shifts left.** Since AD = C + I + G + (X - M), lower C and I shift AD leftward, from AD1 to AD2.

**On the AD/AS diagram:** with the price level on the vertical axis and real GDP on the horizontal axis, the economy starts at an inflationary gap where AD1 crosses SRAS to the RIGHT of the full-employment level (Yf, the LRAS/potential position). The leftward shift to AD2 moves equilibrium back toward Yf: the price level falls from PL1 to PL2 (easing inflation) and real output falls from Y1 toward Yf.

**Judgement note:** the policy reduces inflation but at the cost of lower output and possibly higher unemployment in the short run - the classic trade-off the central bank must weigh.

sections.content: Closing the gaps on the AD/AS diagram
The whole point of demand-side policy is to shift AD to close a gap. Being able to describe the diagram precisely - in words, since these lessons render text - is what earns diagram marks.

items: **Deflationary (recessionary) gap:** short-run equilibrium sits to the LEFT of the full-employment level Yf, so there is spare capacity and unemployment. EXPANSIONARY policy (higher G, lower T, or lower interest rates) shifts AD RIGHT, from AD1 to AD2, moving output up toward Yf. The price level rises somewhat as output recovers.

**Inflationary gap:** short-run equilibrium sits to the RIGHT of Yf; the economy is overheating and the price level is rising. CONTRACTIONARY policy (lower G, higher T, or higher interest rates) shifts AD LEFT, from AD1 to AD2, easing the price level back down toward Yf.

**Always label:** axes (Price Level; Real GDP), the curves (AD, SRAS, and LRAS or Yf for full employment), the shift (AD1 -> AD2) and the change in equilibrium (price level and real output). Then explain in words WHY AD moved - that explanation is the difference between describing and analysing.

sections.content: Evaluating the two policies: strengths and limitations
No demand-side policy is a free lunch. Effectiveness depends on the circumstances, and a strong evaluation weighs each strength against a matching limitation rather than listing them separately.

items: **Fiscal - strengths:** directly and quickly injects demand through G; can be TARGETED at the sectors, regions or groups that need it most; works even when interest rates are already near zero and monetary policy has run out of room.

**Fiscal - limitations:** long DECISION lags (budgets and legislation take time), so the boost can arrive after the slump has passed; deficits add to GOVERNMENT DEBT and future interest costs; CROWDING OUT - borrowing can raise interest rates and squeeze private investment; and it is exposed to POLITICAL constraints (tax rises are unpopular; spending is hard to reverse).

**Monetary - strengths:** decided FAST by an independent committee and easily reversible; free of the political pressure that distorts fiscal choices; credible inflation targeting helps anchor expectations.

**Monetary - limitations:** a BLUNT instrument that hits the whole economy rather than specific sectors; loses traction near the ZERO LOWER BOUND / in a LIQUIDITY TRAP when confidence is low; its IMPACT lag on AD can be many months; and rate rises to curb inflation can raise unemployment (the inflation-output trade-off).
**Mixing up who does what** - interest rates are MONETARY (central bank); spending and taxes are FISCAL (government). Saying 'the government cuts interest rates' throws away easy marks.

**Wrong stance for the situation** - using EXPANSIONARY policy in a boom (or contractionary policy in a recession). Match the stance to the gap: expansionary for a deflationary gap, contractionary for an inflationary gap.
**Naming a policy but not tracing the mechanism** - 'lower interest rates raise AD' with no chain in between. Always explain WHY: cheaper borrowing -> more C and I -> AD shifts.
**Drawing the diagram but not explaining it** - an unlabelled or unexplained AD/AS diagram sits in a lower band. Label axes, curves and the shift, then say in words what it shows.
**One-sided 'evaluation'** - listing only strengths (or only weaknesses). A part (b) answer that lacks balance is capped in the middle bands, however fluent it is.
**Forgetting the multiplier is HL, and misusing it** - SL answers do not need the multiplier formula; HL answers should note that leakages and crowding out shrink its real value.

sections.content: Model answer - marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (b): Evaluate the use of expansionary fiscal policy, rather than monetary policy, to close a deflationary (recessionary) gap. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**MODEL ANSWER**

A deflationary (recessionary) gap exists when short-run equilibrium output lies below the full-employment level (Yf), leaving spare capacity and cyclical unemployment. Both demand-side policies aim to shift aggregate demand (AD = C + I + G + (X - M)) rightward to close the gap; the question is which is better suited to the task.

*Explaining the two policies.* EXPANSIONARY FISCAL POLICY means the government raises its own spending (G) and/or cuts taxes (T). Higher G injects demand directly; lower T raises households' disposable income (raising C) and firms' retained profit (raising I). EXPANSIONARY MONETARY POLICY means the independent central bank lowers the policy interest rate; through the transmission mechanism, cheaper borrowing and less rewarding saving raise C and I, and a weaker exchange rate can lift net exports. Both therefore shift AD to the right.

*Diagram (described).* On an AD/AS diagram with Price Level on the vertical axis and Real GDP on the horizontal axis, the economy begins where AD1 crosses SRAS to the LEFT of the full-employment level Yf (marked by LRAS) - the deflationary gap. Either policy shifts AD from AD1 to AD2; the new equilibrium moves output up toward Yf, with the price level rising modestly from PL1 to PL2. The gap closes as real GDP rises and cyclical unemployment falls.

*Real-world example (developed).* In the 2008-09 global financial crisis, with private demand collapsing and central-bank policy rates cut to near zero, many governments turned to large fiscal stimulus - increased infrastructure spending and temporary tax cuts - precisely because monetary policy had reached its lower limit and could do little more. This illustrates the central issue: fiscal policy can still add demand when interest rates cannot fall further, which is a decisive advantage in a deep recession.

*Evaluation - strengths of fiscal policy here.* Fiscal policy acts DIRECTLY on AD through G, can be TARGETED at the hardest-hit sectors and regions, and - as the example shows - remains effective at the zero lower bound where monetary policy stalls in a LIQUIDITY TRAP. Where confidence is very low, cutting rates further may fail to revive borrowing at all, so fiscal action may be the only tool that works.

*Evaluation - limitations of fiscal policy.* Against this, fiscal policy suffers long DECISION and implementation LAGS, so stimulus may arrive after recovery has begun and prove destabilising; funding a deficit adds to GOVERNMENT DEBT and future interest costs; borrowing can cause CROWDING OUT, raising interest rates and squeezing private investment so the net boost is smaller than the multiplier suggests; and it faces POLITICAL constraints on how quickly spending can rise or fall. Monetary policy, by contrast, is decided FAST, is easily reversible and avoids these political pressures - real strengths when time matters.

*Balanced judgement.* On balance, expansionary fiscal policy is the STRONGER choice specifically in a DEEP recession where interest rates are already near zero and confidence is low - as in 2008-09 - because it can still inject demand when monetary policy cannot, and can be aimed where it is most needed. But this superiority is CONDITIONAL: in a mild slump with room to cut rates, monetary policy's speed and reversibility may make it preferable, and fiscal policy's debt burden and crowding out are real costs. The most effective response is usually the two COORDINATED - expansionary fiscal policy carrying the load while monetary policy keeps borrowing cheap - rather than either used alone. The answer therefore depends on the depth of the gap and the level of interest rates, which is exactly the kind of qualified, evidence-based conclusion the top band demands.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (b), out of 15):**

- **13-15:** the demands of the question are addressed; relevant economic theory is FULLY explained; relevant terms are used appropriately THROUGHOUT; where appropriate, a relevant diagram is included and FULLY explained; evaluation is EFFECTIVE and BALANCED; a relevant real-world example is FULLY DEVELOPED.
- **10-12:** the demands are addressed; theory is explained; terms are used appropriately mostly; a diagram is included and explained; evaluation is MOSTLY balanced; a real-world example is developed.
- **7-9:** the demands are PARTIALLY addressed; theory is PARTLY explained; SOME relevant terms are used; a diagram is included; evaluation LACKS BALANCE; a real-world example is partly developed.
- **4-6:** relevant theory is DESCRIBED; evaluation is SUPERFICIAL; a real-world example is identified.
- **1-3:** the response is NOT RELEVANT to the question; there is NO evaluation.

**Why this answer reaches 13-15:**
- ✅ Addresses the demand - it compares BOTH policies and judges fiscal RATHER THAN monetary, exactly as asked.
- ✅ Theory FULLY explained: each policy's mechanism (G, T, the interest-rate transmission chain) is worked through, not merely named.
- ✅ Terms used THROUGHOUT: deflationary gap, aggregate demand, transmission mechanism, liquidity trap, crowding out, budget deficit.
- ✅ Diagram included AND fully explained: axes, curves, Yf and the AD1 -> AD2 shift are described and their meaning stated.
- ✅ **Real-world example FULLY DEVELOPED** - the 2008-09 use of fiscal stimulus when rates hit the lower bound is not just named but tied directly to the argument, showing WHY fiscal policy was chosen. A bare mention ('e.g. the 2008 crisis') with no link would fall to the 'identified' level and cap the answer.
- ✅ **Evaluation EFFECTIVE and BALANCED** - strengths (direct, targeted, works at the zero bound) are weighed against limitations (lags, debt, crowding out, political constraints), and the conclusion is CONDITIONAL rather than one-sided. An answer that only praised fiscal policy - however fluent - 'lacks balance' and is capped at 7-9.

Those two features - the FULLY DEVELOPED real-world example and the BALANCED, reasoned judgement - are precisely what our marking engine looks for to award the top band.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this 15-mark question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official markband, telling you which band you reached and why. Watch the two issues that most often CAP an otherwise strong Paper 1 part (b): (1) a real-world example that is only NAMED rather than fully developed and linked to your argument, and (2) a ONE-SIDED evaluation. Either one holds you in the 7-9 band no matter how well you explain the theory - so develop the example, and always weigh both sides before you judge.

This lesson advances two of the course's nine key concepts. **Intervention** runs through everything here: fiscal and monetary policy are deliberate government and central-bank actions to correct a market outcome - a slump or an overheating boom - that the economy would not fix quickly on its own. **Change** is the mechanism by which they work: every policy shifts aggregate demand, changing real output, employment and the price level. Keep both in view - the internal assessment rewards linking a key concept to real-world material, and 'intervention' and 'change' recur through every macro policy debate that follows. This content is common to SL and HL; only the multiplier is HL.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (b) evaluation marked: evaluate expansionary fiscal versus monetary policy to close a deflationary gap
